one_line_takeaway: Injecting SHAP-guided dummy code into Mirai's scanning phase evades six power-side-channel ML detectors with up to 96.4% attack success (LSTM), and while adversarial training nearly neutralizes the attack (ASR down to 0.4-1.6% for the strongest variant), naive noise injection leaves reconstruction-based detectors (LSTM-AE) 95%+ vulnerable.
key_finding: Baseline (no attack) classification accuracy on clean traces: BiLSTM+CNN 0.96 and TCN 0.95 were the strongest classifiers (accuracy/precision/recall/F1 all in the 0.94-0.96 range); LSTM (0.64) and LSTM+AE+MLP (0.65) were weakest; BiLSTM 0.91 and CNN+Attention 0.72 were intermediate. Under attack, ASR (Table 4) varied strongly by model and dummy-code variant: LSTM was most vulnerable, reaching 96.4% ASR under 'Two For Loops' and 91.2% under 'One For Loop'; LSTM+AE was also highly vulnerable (up to 89.0% under 'Two For Loops'). CNN+Attention was the most resistant model, with ASR as low as 42.0% (If Statement) and never exceeding ~61.6%; TCN stayed at or below 68% across variants; BiLSTM ranged 50.8-77.3%. The abstract's headline '75.2% average ASR' is the paper's own aggregate figure across all model/variant combinations. Pearson correlation between original and modified traces was lowest (i.e., most perturbed) for Two Nested For Loops (mean r=0.848) [note: table lists 0.848 as the correlation value, described in text as producing the strongest disruption] versus If Statement (r=0.415, but with the highest ANOVA F-statistic of 196.68), indicating correlation and ANOVA divergence do not always move together across variants. Granger causality for 'Two Nested Loops' reached significance levels of 0.5, 0.66, and 0.67 at Lags 1-3 respectively, the strongest and most persistent causal signal of any variant. Defense results (Table 5, own experiments): adversarial training was substantially more effective than noise injection at reducing ASR — e.g., for 'Two Nested Loops' ASR fell to 1.6% (LSTM) and 0.4% (LSTM-AE) after adversarial training, versus 27.6% and 99.6% respectively after noise injection alone; noise injection reduced ASR only modestly for simpler variants (e.g., CNN+Attention on 'If Statement' fell from 78.3% to 47.9%) and left LSTM-AE highly vulnerable (95.5-99.6% residual ASR) across all variants even with noise injection.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a runtime adversarial evasion technique against power side-channel-based IoT malware detectors. The authors modify the open-source Mirai botnet by injecting structured 'dummy code' (benign extra instructions) into its scanning phase to perturb the device's power consumption signature so that AI/ML classifiers misclassify active Mirai infection as benign (Idle or IoT Service) activity, without altering Mirai's core propagation/attack functionality. They use SHAP explainability to identify which temporal segments of the power trace are most discriminative for the 'Mirai' class and time the dummy-code injection to coincide with those high-impact regions. Four dummy-code variants are designed (Single Function, One For Loop, Two Nested For Loops, If Statement), each trading off stealth, disruption strength, and execution overhead. The authors also collect and release (post-publication) a custom power side-channel dataset from five Android smartphones under four states (idle, IoT service, unmodified Mirai, modified Mirai), evaluate the attack against six deep-learning detection architectures, and test two adversarial defenses (adversarial training, targeted noise injection) against their own attack.
threat_model: Gray-box adversary with access to Mirai's open-source code who can modify it pre-deployment to implant dummy operations into the scanning routine; the attacker knows detection relies on time-series power consumption data but has no access to the detector's model internals, parameters, training data, or thresholds. The attacker cannot tamper with the power-monitoring hardware/logging infrastructure and deliberately preserves Mirai's post-infection payload behavior (e.g., DDoS execution) to keep the malware functional. This is explicitly positioned as an application-layer, software-level, remote-feasible threat model, contrasted by the authors against prior side-channel attack work assuming physical access/firmware modification and against white-box models assuming full detector access. Detection architecture assumed: three-tier IoT device -> edge node (runs the ML classifier in real time) -> cloud backend (model retraining/distribution).
limitations: Self-acknowledged by the authors: (1) experiments used Android smartphones as IoT stand-ins in a controlled lab, not embedded Linux/RTOS IoT devices (e.g., ESP32/FreeRTOS), which the authors flag as a generalizability gap for future work; (2) only the Mirai scanning phase was targeted/evaluated -- other botnet stages (infection, C2 communication) were not tested; (3) since the scanning phase executes deterministically per run, longitudinal persistence of evasion over extended operational periods was not directly evaluated; (4) the attack introduces measurable execution-time overhead (especially the 'Two Nested Loops' variant), which the authors themselves note could serve as an auxiliary timing-based detection signal in a multimodal defense, partially undercutting the claimed stealth; (5) background process noise in real-world (non-lab) deployments was not modeled; (6) noise injection as tested used a fixed (non-adaptive) Gaussian profile and was shown by the authors' own results to be much weaker than adversarial training, especially against reconstruction-based models (LSTM-AE).

datasets_used: Custom power side-channel dataset: 5 Android smartphones (Galaxy S23 Ultra, Galaxy S22+, Galaxy A21, Pixel 3, OnePlus), 340 runs/class/device, 1020 samples/device, 5100 total records, 150-sample (150ms) windows at 1kHz sampling, 85/15 train/test split; states = Idle, IoT Service, Mirai (unmodified and dummy-code-modified variants)
CICIDS2017 and BotIoT mentioned only as existing datasets found unsuitable (lack power-consumption data) -- not used in experiments
quantitative_results: Clean-trace baseline accuracy: BiLSTM+CNN 0.96, TCN 0.95, BiLSTM 0.91, CNN+Attention 0.72, LSTM+AE+MLP 0.65, LSTM 0.64
Overall average attack success rate 75.2% (abstract headline figure across models/variants)
ASR by variant/model (Table 4), selected: LSTM 90.0-96.4% across variants (highest 96.4% under Two For Loops); CNN+Attention 42.0-61.6% (lowest, most resistant); TCN <=68%; BiLSTM 50.8-77.3%
Pearson correlation (original vs modified trace): One For Loop 0.647, Two Nested For Loops 0.848, Single Function 0.459, If Statement 0.415
ANOVA F-statistics (p<0.001 for all): One For Loop 19.83, Two Nested For Loops 72.42, Single Function 62.06, If Statement 196.68
Granger causality for Two Nested Loops: significance 0.5 (Lag1), 0.66 (Lag2), 0.67 (Lag3)
Adversarial training defense ASR (Table 5) for Two Nested Loops: LSTM 1.6%, LSTM-AE 0.4% (down from >90% baseline); BiLSTM ASR reduction up to 68 points (Single Function 50.9% -> 18.5%)
Noise injection defense: CNN+Attention If Statement ASR 78.33% -> 47.94%; TCN Single Function 61.76% -> 51.76%; LSTM-AE remained 95.5-99.6% ASR across all variants even with noise injection
baselines_compared: Six detection architectures evaluated as the attacked systems (not prior attack baselines, since this is a novel attack with no prior comparable evasion method cited): LSTM, BiLSTM, TCN, BiLSTM+CNN, CNN+Attention, LSTM Autoencoder+MLP
Two defense baselines tested against the paper's own attack: adversarial training (retraining with dummy-code-perturbed samples) and targeted/fixed Gaussian noise injection at inference time

Prior detection systems DeepPower and DeepAuditor are cited as motivating/related work (the class of systems this attack targets) but are not directly re-implemented or benchmarked in this paper's experiments
